trackTitle: Computer Architecture – From Core Systems to Performance Engineering
description: A complete expert-level learning path covering digital logic, CPU design, memory systems, pipelining, parallelism and real-world performance engineering.

institute.name: Coder & AccoTax
institute.author: Sukanta Hui
institute.location: 25(10/A) Shibtala Road, PO – N. C. Pukur, Barrackpore, West Bengal, India

subject: Computer Architecture

segments.title: Segment 1 – Digital Foundations
segments.summary: Build strong fundamentals of digital systems and number representations.

modules.title: Number Systems & Binary Codes
topics: Range of numbers in n-bit signed systems
ASCII, Unicode, BCD and Gray code
Why 2’s Complement is preferred in computer systems
modules.summary: Understand how computers represent numbers and characters.

modules.title: Logic Gates & Combinational Circuits
topics: Basic gates AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR
Priority encoder (IC 74148 – 8:3, IC 74147 – 10:4 decimal to BCD)
modules.summary: Learn how hardware logic is constructed.

modules.title: Flip‑Flops & Sequential Circuits
topics: Introduction to Sequential Circuits: Difference between combinational vs sequential logic, memory concept, feedback mechanism
SR Latch (Basic Building Block): NOR and NAND implementation, truth table, invalid state, real-world behavior analysis
Gated SR Latch: Enable/clock control, level-sensitive behavior, timing diagram explanation
D Latch: Elimination of invalid state, data transparency concept, practical usage in buffering and storage
Clock Triggering in Flip-Flops: Concept of clock signal, level triggering vs edge triggering (positive and negative edge)
Pulse triggering, timing diagrams, real-world significance in synchronization
Level-Sensitive vs Edge-Triggered Devices: Positive edge, negative edge, timing diagram comparison, why edge triggering is important
Clocked SR Flip-Flop: Internal structure, working with clock pulse, race condition and invalid state problem
D Flip-Flop (Most Important): Circuit design from SR FF, characteristic equation, timing diagram, real-life applications (registers, memory)
JK Flip-Flop (Improved SR FF): Toggle condition, elimination of invalid state, race-around condition and its solution
Master-Slave JK Flip-Flop: Detailed working using two JK flip-flops (Master & Slave), clock phase operation, elimination of race-around condition, timing diagram analysis
T Flip-Flop: Derived from JK FF, toggle operation, frequency division concept (divide-by-2, divide-by-n)
Characteristic Table vs Excitation Table: Conversion methods, exam tricks, how to design circuits using excitation tables
Master-Slave Flip-Flop: Concept of two-stage operation, solving race-around problem, timing control
Flip-Flop Timing Parameters (Very Important): Setup time, hold time, propagation delay, clock skew, metastability
Registers: Concept of data storage, parallel load registers, serial vs parallel data transfer
Shift Registers (Core Topic): SISO, SIPO, PISO, PIPO with diagrams, applications in data transfer and communication
Universal Shift Register (IC 74194): Modes of operation (shift left, shift right, parallel load, hold), real IC understanding
Counters – Introduction: Need for counting circuits, asynchronous vs synchronous concept
Asynchronous (Ripple) Counters: Working, propagation delay issue, timing diagram, disadvantages
Synchronous Counters: Simultaneous triggering, design advantages, speed improvement
Types of Counters: Binary up/down counters, BCD counters (mod-10), ring counter, Johnson counter with applications
Counter Design (Very Important): Step-by-step design using JK and D flip-flops, state table → excitation → circuit
Modulus (MOD) Concept: Designing mod-n counters, truncation techniques
Finite State Machines (FSM): Concept of states, transitions, memory-based systems
Mealy vs Moore Machines: Output dependency, timing differences, practical comparison
State Diagram and State Table: Representation techniques, conversion methods
State Minimization: Reducing number of states using equivalence, optimization techniques
Sequential Circuit Design Procedure: Problem → state diagram → table → flip-flop selection → logic implementation
Timing Diagrams (Critical Skill): Reading and drawing waveforms for flip-flops, registers, and counters
Hazards and Race Conditions: Static and dynamic hazards, race problems in counters, prevention techniques
Real-Life Applications: Frequency divider, digital clock, traffic light controller, sequence detector
Simulation & Tools: Using Logisim / Multisim to design and test flip-flops, registers, and counters
Exam-Oriented Problem Solving: Previous ISC/CBSE style numerical + design questions with shortcuts
learningOutcomes: Design and analyze all common flip‑flop types
Build registers and counters from flip‑flops
Model simple sequential systems with FSMs
modules.summary: Master the building blocks of memory and sequential logic in digital systems.
miniProjects: Build a 4‑bit synchronous counter with display
Design a 3‑bit shift register using D flip‑flops
references: Roth & Kinney – Fundamentals of Logic Design
teachingTips: Use timing diagrams to explain edge triggering
Simulate counters in Logisim or similar tool

segments.title: Segment 2 – CPU Organization & Instruction Cycle
segments.summary: Understand how the processor executes instructions.

modules.title: CPU Organization & Registers
modules.summary: Discover how instructions move inside CPU.

segments.title: Segment 3 – Pipelining, Memory & I/O
segments.summary: Dive into performance-critical components of systems.

modules.title: Pipelining & Hazard Handling
modules.summary: Learn how modern CPUs execute multiple instructions in parallel.

segments.title: Segment 4 – Multiprocessors & Performance Engineering
segments.summary: Master modern computing systems & optimization strategies.

modules.title: Parallel Processing & Multiprocessors
modules.summary: Become capable of reasoning about high-performance computing systems.
